title: Doppler effect for sound waves
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master the Doppler effect for sound waves for Cambridge 9702. Learn the formula, worked examples, and exam tips for P1 and P2.
learningObjectives: Explain qualitatively why the observed frequency of a sound differs from the source frequency when there is relative motion between the source and observer
Use the Doppler equation f_o = f_s × v / (v ± v_s) to calculate observed frequency for a moving source
Identify whether the observed frequency is higher or lower than the source frequency based on the direction of motion of the source

simpleExplanation.title: Why does a siren change pitch as it passes?
simpleExplanation.summary: The Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency of a wave when the source and observer are moving relative to one another. When a source moves towards you, sound waves bunch up, so you hear a higher pitch. As it moves away, the waves spread out and the pitch drops.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine throwing tennis balls at a wall every second while running towards it — the balls pile up closer together and hit the wall more frequently. Run away and they spread out, hitting less often. Sound waves behave in exactly the same way.
steps: Recall that the Doppler effect changes the observed frequency, NOT the wave speed in the medium
Learn the formula f_o = f_s × v / (v ± v_s) and know which sign to use: subtract v_s when the source approaches, add v_s when it recedes
Practise identifying the direction of motion from context clues in the question — ambulances, aircraft, and trains are favourite Cambridge scenarios
Check your answer makes physical sense: approaching source → f_o > f_s; receding source → f_o < f_s

sections.content: You have almost certainly heard the Doppler effect without realising it — that swooping drop in pitch as a fast car or ambulance rushes past. Cambridge 9702 expects you to explain this qualitatively AND calculate the **observed frequency** using the Doppler equation. This topic appears regularly in both **Paper 1 multiple-choice** and **Paper 2 structured questions**, so a solid understanding here is worth easy marks.

The **Doppler effect** is the change in the **observed (received) frequency** of a wave caused by relative motion between the source of the wave and the observer. It applies to all waves — sound, light, water — but Cambridge 9702 topic 7.3 focuses specifically on sound.

Key principle: the **speed of sound in the medium is unchanged** by the motion of the source. What changes is the spacing of the wavefronts (the wavelength), and therefore the rate at which they arrive at the observer (the observed frequency).

• Source moving **towards** observer → wavefronts are **compressed** → shorter wavelength → **higher** observed frequency
• Source moving **away from** observer → wavefronts are **stretched** → longer wavelength → **lower** observed frequency

f_o = f_s × v / (v ± v_s)

Where:
  f_o = observed (received) frequency (Hz)
  f_s = frequency emitted by the source (Hz)
  v   = speed of sound in the medium (m s⁻¹)
  v_s = speed of the source (m s⁻¹)

Sign rule:
  Use (v − v_s) when the source moves TOWARDS the observer  → denominator smaller → f_o > f_s ✓
  Use (v + v_s) when the source moves AWAY from the observer → denominator larger  → f_o < f_s ✓

**Important note:** This form of the equation assumes the **observer is stationary**. Cambridge 9702 AS Level only requires you to deal with a moving source and a stationary observer, so you will always use this version of the formula. Do not confuse v (wave speed) with v_s (source speed) — they are completely different quantities.

items: The Doppler effect arises from relative motion between source and observer — it is the wavefronts that bunch up or spread out, not the wave speeding up or slowing down
The speed of sound in air (≈ 340 m s⁻¹) is determined by the medium, not the motion of the source — examiners frequently test this misconception
Use (v − v_s) in the denominator when the source approaches; use (v + v_s) when it recedes — getting this the wrong way round is the single most common error in P2
If v_s = 0 (stationary source), f_o = f_s — the formula correctly reduces to no Doppler shift
The observed wavelength also changes: λ_o = v / f_o — Cambridge may ask you to calculate this as a follow-up part
A qualitative description must mention: wavefronts, compression or extension of spacing, and the resulting change in frequency — include all three for full marks

sections.question:
An ambulance emits a siren at a constant frequency of 840 Hz. It travels in a straight line at 30 m s⁻¹ towards a stationary observer standing at the roadside. The speed of sound in air is 340 m s⁻¹.

(a) Calculate the frequency heard by the observer as the ambulance approaches.
(b) Calculate the frequency heard by the observer after the ambulance has passed and is moving away.
(c) State and explain whether the speed of sound heard by the observer changes as the ambulance passes.
sections.solution:
Part (a) — Ambulance approaching:

Use f_o = f_s × v / (v − v_s)   [subtract because source moves TOWARDS observer]

f_o = 840 × 340 / (340 − 30)
f_o = 840 × 340 / 310
f_o = 285 600 / 310
f_o = 921 Hz  (3 s.f.)

The observer hears a higher pitch than 840 Hz. ✓

---

Part (b) — Ambulance receding:

Use f_o = f_s × v / (v + v_s)   [add because source moves AWAY from observer]

f_o = 840 × 340 / (340 + 30)
f_o = 840 × 340 / 370
f_o = 285 600 / 370
f_o = 772 Hz  (3 s.f.)

The observer hears a lower pitch than 840 Hz. ✓

---

Part (c) — Speed of sound:

The speed of sound does NOT change. The speed of a sound wave is determined by the properties of the medium (air), not by the motion of the source. The observer always receives sound travelling at 340 m s⁻¹ through the air, regardless of whether the ambulance is approaching or receding.

sections.content: Cambridge mark schemes award a specific mark for the sign convention in the Doppler equation. Always write out your substitution in full — e.g. write '340 − 30' or '340 + 30' clearly — so the examiner can see you chose the correct denominator even if you make an arithmetic slip. Never leave it ambiguous. Also, if a question asks you to 'explain' the Doppler effect, you must use the word 'wavefront' or refer to the spacing of the waves — vague answers about 'the sound bunches up' without linking it to wavelength and frequency will not score full marks.
Qualitative Explanation — How to Write It for Exam Credit

Many students can use the formula but struggle to write a clear qualitative explanation. Here is a model structure Cambridge rewards:

1. **As the source moves towards the observer**, each successive wavefront is emitted from a position closer to the observer than the previous one.
2. This causes the wavefronts to be **closer together** (the wavelength is reduced) in the region between the source and the observer.
3. Because the wave speed in air is unchanged, a shorter wavelength means the wavefronts arrive **more frequently**.
4. Therefore, the **observed frequency is higher** than the emitted frequency.

Reverse the argument for a receding source. Practise writing this out until it feels natural — it is worth 3–4 marks in a structured question.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: How do I remember which sign to use in the Doppler effect formula for Cambridge 9702?
faq.a: A reliable trick: think about what the motion does to the denominator. When the source approaches, the wavefronts bunch up, so the effective denominator should be smaller — that means you subtract v_s from v. A smaller denominator gives a larger f_o, which is physically correct (higher pitch). When the source recedes, add v_s to make the denominator larger and f_o smaller. Our free course includes colour-coded formula cards and past-paper practice questions to help this stick.

faq.q: Does the speed of sound change due to the Doppler effect in Cambridge 9702?
faq.a: No — this is one of Cambridge's favourite misconceptions to test. The speed of sound depends only on the medium (temperature, density, elasticity of air), not on the motion of the source. The Doppler effect changes the observed frequency and wavelength, never the wave speed. Examiners reward students who state this explicitly. You will find questions testing this exact point in the 9702 past papers available on our site.

faq.q: Is the Doppler effect in 9702 only for sound, or does it apply to light as well?
faq.a: Cambridge 9702 topic 7.3 focuses specifically on sound waves, and the equation f_o = f_s × v / (v ± v_s) is only required for sound with a moving source and stationary observer at AS Level. Light also exhibits a Doppler effect (used in astronomy to measure star velocities via redshift), but that is covered in A Level topic 15 and requires a different relativistic treatment. For P1 and P2 purposes, keep your Doppler calculations to sound. Our free 9702 revision lessons cover both AS and A Level topics in full.
